mainEntity.name: How much does a Mercedes C200 or C220 CDI engine replacement cost in the UK?

acceptedAnswer.text: A reconditioned OM611 or OM646 engine typically costs £2,800–£5,000 supply only. Used engines are available from £1,200–£2,800. A replacement OM646.963 engine costs approximately £1,246 for a used unit in good condition. Mercedes C-Class engine replacements typically cost £3,500 or more, with total costs (including labour) potentially exceeding £5,000. Fitting adds £1,000–£1,600 depending on your location and workshop. Main dealer new engines are no longer available.

mainEntity.name: Is the Mercedes C200 or C220 CDI engine reliable?

acceptedAnswer.text: The OM646 in particular is widely regarded as a 'very durable engine'. However, these engines have known weak points. The most notorious is injector seal failure ('black death'), where leaking injector seals allow unburnt diesel and exhaust gases to escape. Other common issues include turbocharger problems (sticking vanes), timing chain tensioner wear, and DPF clogging (on W203/W204 models). With proper maintenance, these engines can be very reliable.

mainEntity.name: What are the common problems with the Mercedes C200 and C220 CDI?

acceptedAnswer.text: The most frequently reported issues are diesel injector seal failure ('black death' — soot around injectors, chuffing noise, exhaust smell inside cabin), turbocharger issues (loss of power, limp mode, sticking vanes), timing chain tensioner wear (rattling on cold start), and DPF clogging (urban use). The specific issues depend on which generation and engine you have.

mainEntity.name: Is it worth replacing a Mercedes C200 or C220 CDI engine, or should I scrap the car?

acceptedAnswer.text: Depends on vehicle value and condition. Against a well-kept £5,000 C220 CDI, a £4,000 recon is 80% — exceeding our 70% Mercedes threshold. Against a tired £2,500 example, the same recon significantly exceeds the threshold — get a scrap valuation first. Well-maintained C-Class CDIs are becoming rarer — a proper restoration is often justified for a clean example.

mainEntity.name: How do I find my Mercedes C200 or C220 CDI engine code?

acceptedAnswer.text: Listed on the VIN plate and vehicle documentation. W202 (1997–2000): OM611. W203 (2000–2007): OM611 (early) or OM646 (late). W204 (2007–2010): OM646. The badge on the tailgate is the easiest tell — 'C200 CDI' or 'C220 CDI'.

mainEntity.name: Can I repair the injector seals on the OM611/OM646, or does it need full engine replacement?

acceptedAnswer.text: Yes — injector seal replacement is a well-understood repair costing £400–£1,200. The term 'black death' refers to the carbon buildup around injectors when seals fail. If caught early, the copper seals can be replaced and the carbon cleaned. If left untreated, the injector can seize in the cylinder head, requiring cylinder head removal or replacement. If you notice a chuffing noise or smell of exhaust gases inside the cabin, have the injector seals inspected immediately.

eraMap.title: The C200/C220 CDI THROUGH TIME — Era Map

rows.eraNote: Mercedes' first common-rail diesel in the C-Class. Fixed-vane turbo; injector seal failure ("black death") is a known pattern.
The most common C-Class CDI generation. OM646 is a durable engine; injector seals, turbo vanes, and DPF are the main concerns.
The final 2.1-litre CDI. Updated common-rail system; injector seal failure and juddering on cold start are known patterns.

eraMap.sourceNote: This table is the single source every other section on this page matches — Sections 2, 4 and 6 reference it rather than restating conflicting figures.

replacementCosts.h2: Mercedes C200/C220 CDI Engine Replacement Costs

rows.bestFor: No longer available from Mercedes — the OM611/OM646 are out of production

replacementCosts.figuresNote: Figures above reflect the OM611 and OM646 2.1-litre diesel engines. A replacement OM646.963 engine costs approximately £1,246 for a used unit in good condition, with reconditioned engines costing up to £1,495. Mercedes C-Class engine replacements typically cost £3,500 or more, with total costs (including labour) potentially exceeding £5,000. Main dealer new engines are no longer available.
replacementCosts.labourEstimate: Fitting a C-Class CDI engine typically takes 10–14 hours at £70–£100/hr (independent) or £120–£180/hr (London/SE dealer).

cta.label: → Compare C-Class diesel engine prices from UK specialists

problems.title: Diesel Injector Seal Failure ("Black Death") — OM611 & OM646
problems.symptoms: Chuffing or ticking noise from the engine bay, smell of exhaust gases inside the cabin, soot buildup around injectors, rough running, loss of power, engine warning light.
problems.repairCost: £400–£1,200 (injector seal replacement — copper seals + cleaning)【PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet】
problems.replacementCost: £2,500–£6,500 (if left untreated and damages the cylinder head)
problems.recommendation: "Black death" is the most notorious issue on Mercedes OM611 and OM646 diesel engines. When injector seals fail, unburnt diesel and exhaust gases escape past the injector, causing carbon buildup around the injector. If left untreated, the injector can seize in the cylinder head, requiring cylinder head removal or replacement. If you notice a chuffing noise or smell of exhaust gases inside the cabin, have the injector seals inspected immediately. Replacement of the copper seals and cleaning of carbon deposits is the solution.

failureLink.label: Read the full injector seal failure guide

problems.title: Turbocharger Issues — Sticking Vanes / Boost Pressure Faults (OM611 & OM646)
problems.symptoms: Loss of power, limp mode (engine won't rev past 2000 RPM), black smoke, whistling or changed turbo sound, intermittent loss of power.
problems.repairCost: £500–£1,500 (turbo actuator or turbocharger replacement)【PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet】
problems.replacementCost: £2,500–£6,500 (if turbo failure leads to engine damage)
problems.recommendation: Common turbocharger issues on the OM611 and OM646 include jammed turbo vanes, air flow sensor faults, blocked EGR valves, boost pressure sensor faults, or vacuum leaks to the turbo control valve. If you experience loss of power or limp mode, have the turbo system inspected. A sticking turbo actuator can often be cleaned or replaced without replacing the entire turbocharger.

failureLink.label: Read the full turbocharger failure guide

problems.title: Timing Chain Tensioner Wear
problems.symptoms: Rattling noise on cold start lasting a few seconds, metallic ticking from the engine, engine warning light, eventual catastrophic failure if ignored.
problems.repairCost: £800–£2,000 (timing chain + tensioner replacement)【PLACEHOLDER — confirm vs cost sheet】
problems.replacementCost: £2,500–£6,500 (if chain snaps and pistons strike valves)

urgency.label: Immediate
urgency.text: if you hear rattle, book an inspection today

problems.recommendation: Timing chain tensioner wear is a known issue on these engines. A rattling noise on cold start that disappears after a few seconds indicates the tensioner is losing pressure overnight — an early sign of chain stretch. If ignored, the chain can skip or snap, causing catastrophic engine damage. At the first sign of cold-start rattle, book an inspection immediately. Regular oil changes using MB 229.5 or 229.51 spec oil are essential.

repairBuyOrReplace.h2: C200/C220 CDI — Repair, Buy, or Replace?

rows.whenItMakesSense: Well-understood fix; catch it early before the injector seizes
Actuator replacement or cleaning is cheaper than full turbo replacement
Preventative — catches the fault before catastrophic failure
Forced regeneration or DPF clean — less than replacement
Typically requires full engine replacement or complete rebuild

buyingChecks.title: Buying This C-Class CDI? Check These First
buyIf: * Full Mercedes service history with evidence of regular oil changes using MB 229.5 or 229.51 spec oil
* Injector seals replaced or inspected within the last 40,000 miles
* No cold-start rattle — a quiet startup suggests healthy timing chain
* Post-2003 OM646 preferred over early OM611 (more durable)
avoidIf: * Chuffing noise or exhaust smell in the cabin (injector seal failure)
* Cold-start rattle with no timing chain history
* Loss of power or limp mode with no turbo diagnosis
* Poor service history — these engines are durable but need good maintenance

repairBuyOrReplace.closingVerdict: The C200 and C220 CDI badges represent Mercedes' first common-rail diesel engines in the C-Class — a significant technological leap that delivered excellent fuel economy and refined performance. The OM646 in particular is widely regarded as a "very durable engine". However, these engines have known weak points: injector seal failure ("black death") is the most notorious; turbocharger issues and timing chain tensioner wear are also common. These are well-understood and fixable issues, but they demand attention and budget. Buy on documented history, not on faith. A well-maintained C-Class CDI is a £3,000–£8,000 used premium saloon; a neglected one is a £4,000 engine replacement waiting to happen.

cta.label: → Compare C-Class diesel engine prices before you buy

buyingChecklist.intro: Before you buy a used Mercedes C200 or C220 CDI (or before you commit to an engine replacement), check these:
items: * Cold Start — Listen for rattling (timing chain tensioner warning) or chuffing (injector seal warning)
* Service History — including which engine era (OM611 or OM646) and confirmed oil-change intervals using MB 229.5/229.51 spec oil
* Injector Seal Check — Any history of injector seal replacement? Check for soot around injectors
* Turbo Check — Any loss of power, limp mode, or black smoke? (turbo vanes or actuator warning)
* DPF Check (W203/W204) — Any DPF warning lights or regeneration issues? (urban-driven vehicles are high risk)
* Smoke Check — Black smoke can indicate turbo or injector issues; white smoke can indicate coolant issues
* Engine Warning Light — Have codes been read? Any unresolved injector, turbo, or timing-related codes?
* MOT History — Check for emissions-related failures
